content.full_text:
February 1, 1990

Mr. Scott Hallabrin  
Counsel, Legal Division  
Fair Political Practices Commission  
P.O. Box 807  
428 "J" Street, Suite 800  
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Re: Opinion on Campaign Funds and Recall Elections  
(Your File No. A-89-358)

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

I have just read the above opinion and, without researching the matter, question whether the potential constitutional conflict between Government Code Section 85300 and Article II, Section 18 of the California Constitution really exists. The text of Section 85300, as set forth on page 3 of your letter to Senator Roberti, prohibits expenditure by a public officer of public monies "for the purpose of seeking elective office." (Underscoring added.)

A public officer defending against an attempted recall is in no sense seeking public office. Rather, he is in public office, the exact circumstance that is a necessary prerequisite to the recall effort against him.

Since Elections Code Sections 27333, as to city officers, and 27341(3), as to all other public officers, prohibit a recalled officer from succeeding himself at the recall election, the public officer is necessarily not a candidate at that election within the meaning of Section 85300.

In actuality, recall elections are both a measure and an election to office, in that the first question is the measure, in the form of whether the incumbent should be summarily removed from office, while the second question is an election to determine who is to succeed the recalled officer.

Very truly yours,

David R. Frank  
County Counsel
